front.title: Forward Error Correction for Media over QUIC (MoQ)

abstract.t: This document specifies a mechanism for transmitting Forward Error Correction (FEC) repair data alongside source media in Media over QUIC (MoQ) sessions. It defines signaling for FEC configuration via both control messages and catalog extensions, conventions for repair track naming, and the format of repair objects. The mechanism supports RaptorQ (RFC 6330), Reed-Solomon (RFC 5510), and XOR-based FEC schemes, enabling receivers to recover from packet loss without retransmission latency. A repair container abstraction allows repair objects to carry either condensed-framed symbols or container-specific payloads (e.g., MMTP repair packets), enabling multi-path FEC delivery where receivers apply identical recovery logic regardless of whether data arrives via MoQ/QUIC, SSM multicast, or ATSC 3.0 broadcast. This specification is designed for compatibility with ATSC 3.0 (A/331) and ARIB STD-B60 broadcast systems.

section.name: Introduction
section.t: Media over QUIC (MoQ) provides a publish/subscribe transport for real-time media. While QUIC provides reliable delivery through retransmission, the latency incurred by retransmission can be unacceptable for ultra-low-latency (ULL) applications such as live sports, gaming, and interactive broadcasts.

Forward Error Correction (FEC) enables receivers to recover lost packets using redundant repair data, without waiting for retransmissions. This is particularly valuable in wireless environments where burst packet loss is common.
This document defines:

ol.li: A FEC_CONFIG message for signaling FEC parameters per subscription
An alternative FEC_CONFIG extension header for alignment with MoQ transport extension mechanisms
A catalog extension for out-of-band FEC signaling
A naming convention for repair tracks
The format of repair objects containing FEC symbols
Interleaving strategies to protect against burst loss
Compatibility mappings for ATSC 3.0 and ARIB STD-B60 broadcast systems
Multi-path delivery where FEC symbols arrive from multiple transport paths (MoQ/QUIC, SSM multicast, ATSC 3.0 broadcast) with identical recovery at the receiver

section.t: The mechanism is designed to complement existing MoQ media packaging formats including CMAF , LOC , and MMT by operating as a separate protection layer.

section.name: Terminology
section.t: The key words "MUST", "MUST NOT", "REQUIRED", "SHALL", "SHALL NOT", "SHOULD", "SHOULD NOT", "RECOMMENDED", "NOT RECOMMENDED", "MAY", and "OPTIONAL" in this document are to be interpreted as described in BCP 14 when, and only when, they appear in all capitals, as shown here.

dl.dd: A MoQ track carrying media data (e.g., video, audio).
A MoQ track carrying FEC repair symbols for a corresponding source track.
A sequence of K source symbols over which FEC encoding is performed.
A fixed-size unit of source data used for FEC encoding.
A symbol generated by the FEC encoder that can be used to recover lost source symbols.
The combination of K source symbols and P repair symbols.
The number of media units (e.g., frames, chunks) spanned by a single source block.
Parameters required to configure a RaptorQ decoder, as defined in .
A zero-based index identifying a specific source block within a session.

section.name: Protocol Overview
section.t: The protocol operates as follows:

ol.li: Subscriber sends SUBSCRIBE for source track
Publisher responds with SUBSCRIBE_OK
Publisher MAY send FEC_CONFIG to indicate FEC availability
Subscriber sends SUBSCRIBE for repair track if FEC desired
Publisher sends source objects followed by repair objects
Subscriber uses repair symbols to recover any lost source symbols

section.t: Alternatively, FEC parameters MAY be discovered via catalog () before subscribing.

section.name: FEC_CONFIG Message
section.t: The FEC_CONFIG message is sent by the publisher after SUBSCRIBE_OK to advertise FEC parameters. Subscribers use this information to decide whether to subscribe to the repair track.
section.artwork: FEC_CONFIG Message { Message Type (i) = 0x50, Subscribe ID (i), FEC Enabled (1), FEC Algorithm (8), Repair Container (8), Source Symbols Per Block (i), Repair Symbols Per Block (i), Symbol Size (i), Interleave Depth (i), OTI Length (i), Object Transmission Information (..), }

dl.dd: The subscription this FEC configuration applies to.
1 if FEC is available for this track, 0 otherwise.
The FEC algorithm identifier (see ).

Identifies the format of repair object payloads (see ). Default value 0x00 indicates the condensed repair format defined in (raw repair symbol, SBN/ESI derived from MoQ framing). Value 0x01 indicates MMTP repair packet passthrough per . Receivers branch once on this value at subscription time to select the appropriate byte-offset extraction recipe.
Number of source symbols per FEC block. MUST be >= 1.
Number of repair symbols generated per block. MUST be >= 1.
Size of each symbol in bytes. All symbols in a block MUST have the same size. For RaptorQ, MUST be a multiple of the Symbol Alignment parameter (typically 8 bytes per RFC 6330).
Number of media units (frames/chunks) spanned by one source block. Higher values protect against longer burst losses but increase latency.
Length of the OTI field in bytes. 0 if not applicable.
Algorithm-specific parameters. For RaptorQ, this is the concatenation of the 8-byte Common FEC OTI and the 4-byte Scheme-Specific FEC OTI (12 bytes total) as defined in Sections 3.3.2 and 3.3.3.

section.name: FEC_CONFIG Extension Header (Provisional)
section.t: NOTE: This section is provisional and depends on a future extension to that adds extension header support on control messages (e.g., SUBSCRIBE_OK). As of moq-transport-15, extension headers are defined only for Object payloads, not control messages. Implementations MUST use the FEC_CONFIG message () as the normative signaling mechanism until such an extension is adopted.
For implementations where MoQ transport supports control message extensions, FEC parameters MAY alternatively be conveyed as an extension header in SUBSCRIBE_OK:
section.artwork: FEC_CONFIG Extension Header { Extension Type (i) = 0x50, Length (i), FEC Enabled (1), FEC Algorithm (8), Source Symbols Per Block (i), Repair Symbols Per Block (i), Symbol Size (i), Interleave Depth (i), OTI Length (i), Object Transmission Information (..), }
section.t: Relays MUST cache and forward this extension header without modification.
The FEC_CONFIG message () is the normative mechanism. The extension header is informational and provided for convenience. Publishers MUST NOT send both simultaneously for the same subscription.

dl.dd: Simple XOR-based FEC. Each repair symbol is the XOR of all source symbols in the block. Can recover exactly one lost symbol per block.
RaptorQ fountain code per . Can recover from loss of any symbols as long as K symbols (source or repair) are received. Recommended for most applications.
Reed-Solomon erasure code over GF(2^8) per . Can recover up to P lost symbols. Suitable for applications requiring exact recovery guarantees or interoperability with ATSC 3.0 systems using RS FEC.

section.name: RaptorQ Object Transmission Information
section.t: When FEC Algorithm is RaptorQ (0x02), the OTI field contains the concatenation of the 8-byte Common FEC OTI (Section 3.3.2 of ) and the 4-byte Scheme-Specific FEC OTI (Section 3.3.3 of ), totaling 12 bytes:
section.artwork: RaptorQ OTI { Transfer Length (40), Reserved (8), Symbol Size (16), Number of Source Blocks (8), Number of Sub-Blocks (16), Symbol Alignment (8), }
section.t: Note: For MoQ usage, Transfer Length is typically computed per source block from the cumulative size of source objects in the block.

section.name: Reed-Solomon Parameters
section.t: When FEC Algorithm is Reed-Solomon (0x03), the OTI field contains:

section.name: Repair Object Format Selection
section.t: The format of repair object payloads is determined by the repair track's packaging type in the catalog. Each packaging type defines its own repair object layout:

tr.td: Raw repair symbol, SBN/ESI from MoQ framing ()
LOC object with repair symbol payload ()

section.t: The repair track's packaging MUST match the source track's packaging when the source uses a format with native FEC support (mmtp, loc). When the source uses cmaf packaging, the repair track uses condensed packaging because CMAF does not define a native repair framing.
Receivers determine the repair format by inspecting the repair track's packaging field in the catalog. No separate "repair container" signaling is needed -- the packaging field IS the signal.

section.name: Catalog Fields
section.t: MoQ catalogs MAY include FEC configuration at the track level:

The repair track is a regular catalog track whose packaging field indicates the repair object format (). In this example, both source and repair use mmtp packaging -- the MMTP type 0x03 repair packet format carries FEC Payload ID and OTI natively.
Field definitions for the "fec" object:

ul.li: "none": No FEC
"xor": Simple XOR
"raptorq": RaptorQ per RFC 6330
"reed-solomon": Reed-Solomon per RFC 5510

dl.dd: K value - source symbols per FEC block.
P value - repair symbols per FEC block.
T value - bytes per symbol.
Number of media units per FEC block. Default is 1.
Track name for repair symbols, following the convention in .

section.name: Relationship to FEC_CONFIG
section.t: The FEC_CONFIG message () is the normative signaling mechanism. The catalog FEC extension () provides out-of-band discovery before subscription.
Precedence rules when multiple signaling sources are present:

ol.li: FEC_CONFIG message is authoritative. If present, it defines the FEC parameters for the subscription.
Catalog FEC fields are informational for discovery purposes. Subscribers use catalog data to decide whether to subscribe to repair tracks before receiving FEC_CONFIG.
If FEC_CONFIG parameters differ from catalog values, subscribers MUST use FEC_CONFIG parameters for decoding.
Subscribers SHOULD log a warning if FEC_CONFIG differs from catalog, as this may indicate a configuration error.
The provisional extension header () is informational only and MUST NOT be used as the sole FEC signaling mechanism.

section.t: For multicast delivery where FEC_CONFIG cannot be sent (no back channel), catalog-based signaling is REQUIRED.
Relay behavior: Relays MUST forward FEC_CONFIG messages unmodified to downstream subscribers. Relays MUST NOT strip FEC_CONFIG in favor of catalog or extension header signaling.

section.name: Track Naming
section.t: For a source track with name components [namespace, track_name], the corresponding repair track MUST be named:
section.artwork: [namespace, track_name, "repair"]
section.t: Examples:
This convention allows subscribers to discover repair tracks without additional signaling.

section.name: Subscription Model
section.t: Subscribers SHOULD subscribe to the source track first, then subscribe to the repair track if:

ol.li: The FEC_CONFIG message indicates FEC is available, AND
The subscriber desires FEC protection

section.t: Publishers MUST NOT require subscription to repair tracks. Repair tracks are optional and subscribers MAY choose to rely solely on QUIC retransmission.
Subscribers MAY subscribe to repair tracks without first receiving FEC_CONFIG if they have out-of-band knowledge of FEC availability (e.g., from catalog).

section.name: Condensed Repair Format
section.t: This section defines the condensed repair format, used when the repair track's packaging is "condensed" in the catalog. This is the default repair format for CMAF source tracks, which do not have a native FEC framing.
When the repair track uses a different packaging (e.g., "mmtp" per , or "loc" per ), repair object payloads follow the packaging-specific format and this section does not apply.
Each MoQ object on a repair track contains exactly one repair symbol. The object payload is the raw repair symbol data (Symbol Size bytes), with no additional header.
SBN and ESI are derived from MoQ transport framing:
section.artwork: SBN = floor(group_id / interleave_depth) ESI = K + object_id
section.t: where K (source symbols per block) and interleave_depth are known from the catalog or FEC_CONFIG. Repair symbols have ESI >= K by convention: object_id 0 on the repair track corresponds to ESI K, object_id 1 to ESI K+1, and so on up to object_id P-1 (ESI K+P-1).

section.name: Repair Track Group Structure
section.t: Publishers MUST structure repair tracks so that group_id equals the Source Block Number (SBN). Object IDs within each repair group MUST be sequential starting from 0, where object_id R corresponds to repair symbol ESI K+R. This convention enables receivers to derive SBN and ESI from MoQ framing without per-object metadata, regardless of the source track's container format (LOC, CMAF, etc.).

This convention holds for all container modes. While CMAF source objects require explicit FEC Payload ID extensions () because CMAF chunks do not map 1:1 to source symbols, repair objects ARE 1:1 with symbols (each object = one T-byte repair symbol), so the derivation is valid.

Publishers MUST publish exactly one repair symbol per MoQ object. Bundling multiple repair symbols in a single object would create a single point of failure -- losing that object loses all repair for the block, defeating the purpose of FEC. Separate objects allow independent delivery, priority, and congestion handling per symbol.
The number of repair symbols per block (P) is known from the catalog or FEC_CONFIG and does not need to be signaled per object.

section.name: Repair Symbol Generation
section.t: For RaptorQ, repair symbols are generated per Section 5.3. The Encoding Symbol ID (ESI) for repair symbols starts at K (the number of source symbols).
For Reed-Solomon, repair symbols are generated per using the Vandermonde matrix construction.
For XOR, a single repair symbol is generated as:
section.artwork: repair_symbol = source_symbol[0] XOR source_symbol[1] XOR ... XOR source_symbol[K-1]

section.name: LOC Repair Format
section.t: When the repair track's packaging is "loc", each MoQ object on the repair track is a standard LOC object containing one repair symbol as its payload. SBN and ESI are derived from MoQ transport framing identically to the condensed format ():
section.artwork: SBN = floor(group_id / interleave_depth) ESI = K + object_id
section.t: LOC repair objects MAY include an Auth Tag extension header for per-symbol authentication. No FEC-specific extension headers are needed -- the LOC framing plus MoQ object coordinates provide all metadata required for FEC recovery.
LOC repair is the natural pairing for LOC source tracks. Both source and repair use the same LOC packaging, enabling receivers to apply a single LOC parser to both tracks.

section.name: Condensed Multicast Wire Format
section.t: When condensed-packaged content is delivered over IP multicast (SSM/ASM), each UDP datagram carries one condensed multicast packet. This format provides audio/video demuxing (Track ID), FEC metadata (SBN/ESI), and optional authentication -- all catalog-driven with no per-packet flags.
section.artwork: Condensed Multicast Packet { Track ID (16), // 2 bytes -- audio/video routing Repair (8), // 1 byte -- 0=source, 1=repair Source Block Number (24), // 3 bytes -- SBN (wraps at ~16M blocks) Encoding Symbol ID (8), // 1 byte -- ESI (max 255 symbols/block) Auth Length (8), // 1 byte -- 0=no auth, else tag size Payload (..), // Source chunk or repair symbol [Auth Tag (Auth Length)], // Variable-length (HMAC, ALTA, etc.) }

dl.dd: Identifies the media track within the SSM group. Assigned per-track by the publisher and signaled in the catalog. Receivers use Track ID to route packets to the correct decoder (e.g., video vs audio). Analogous to MMTP packet_id.

0 for source packets, 1 for repair symbols. Receivers branch on this field to route source data to the media decoder and repair data to the FEC recovery engine. The explicit flag is necessary because K may vary per block (e.g., blocks spanning keyframes), making ESI >= K unreliable for source/repair discrimination.
Source Block Number. Identifies the FEC block. Wraps at 16,777,216 -- at 30fps with interleave depth 4 (~7.5 blocks/sec), this provides ~25 days before wraparound.
Encoding Symbol ID. For source packets, ESI < K. For repair packets, ESI >= K. Maximum 255 symbols per block (e.g., K=247 source + P=8 repair).

Length of the authentication tag in bytes. 0 if no authentication for this packet. Maximum 255 bytes, sufficient for HMAC-SHA256 (32), Ed25519 (64), and ALTA variable-length tags (chained MACs + optional signature). The per-packet length field is necessary because schemes like ALTA produce variable-size tags depending on MAC chain depth and signature presence.
Source chunk data (CMAF moof+mdat fragment or portion thereof) or repair symbol (Symbol Size bytes from FEC encoding).
Authentication tag. Present when Auth Length > 0. Format is scheme-specific (HMAC, ALTA, etc.) as declared in the catalog.

section.t: Fixed overhead: 8 bytes. Compare to MMTP repair (33 bytes) and LOC with extensions (~14+ bytes). The 4x reduction in per-packet overhead is significant for high-rate multicast streams.
On the MoQ unicast path, condensed packaging uses the headerless format () with SBN/ESI derived from MoQ transport framing. The multicast wire format in this section applies only to UDP delivery.

section.name: MoQ Extension Headers for FEC
section.t: This section defines MoQ transport extension headers used by FEC-enabled streams. These extensions use the MoQ transport extension header mechanism defined in . Each extension is self-describing (type + length). Presence is determined by the catalog -- no per-object flags byte is needed.

section.name: FEC Payload ID Extension
section.t: The FEC Payload ID extension carries the combined Source Block Number (SBN, 32 bits) and Encoding Symbol ID (ESI, 32 bits) for a total of 8 bytes.

This extension is REQUIRED on CMAF source track objects when FEC is enabled. CMAF chunks are variable-size due to VBR encoding, and the object-to-source-block mapping is not 1:1 -- a single CMAF chunk may span multiple source blocks, or multiple chunks may fall within one block. The explicit SBN+ESI enables receivers to correctly associate each object with its FEC source block.
This extension is NOT needed on LOC source objects. For LOC, SBN and ESI are derivable from MoQ object coordinates:
section.artwork: SBN = floor(group_id / interleave_depth) ESI = object_id
section.t: This derivation works for all cases including late join and relay forwarding because the catalog (which contains interleave_depth) is always received before the first media object, and group_id/object_id are present in every MoQ object header.
This extension is NOT present on repair track objects. Repair objects derive SBN and ESI from MoQ framing (): SBN = floor(group_id / interleave_depth), ESI = K + object_id.

section.name: Object Length Extension
section.t: The Object Length extension carries the original object size as a QUIC variable-length integer (1-8 bytes, per Section 16). The encoding is self-describing: the two most significant bits of the first byte indicate the field width.

This extension is REQUIRED on CMAF source track objects when FEC is enabled. The receiver needs the original object size to strip zero-padding from the last FEC source symbol. CMAF chunks are variable-size (VBR encoding), and a single object may span multiple source blocks, making the padding length non-trivial to derive without this field.
This extension is NOT needed on LOC source objects. MoQ framing combined with fixed symbol size is sufficient to determine original object boundaries and strip padding.

section.name: Auth Tag Extension
section.t: The Auth Tag extension carries an authentication tag whose length is given by the MoQ extension Length field. Tag size may be fixed (HMAC-SHA256: always 32 bytes) or variable (ALTA : varies per packet depending on MAC chain depth and signature presence). The catalog declares the auth scheme and key distribution mechanism; the per-object Length field carries the actual tag size.
This extension is OPTIONAL on LOC source and repair track objects. MMTP tracks use MMTP-native authentication. Condensed multicast packets carry auth via the Auth Length field ().

The Auth Tag provides end-to-end content authentication across untrusted relays (e.g., ALTA). MoQ transport security (TLS/QUIC) protects individual hops but does not protect content integrity across the full delivery chain -- a compromised or malicious relay could modify object payloads before re-encrypting for the next hop. The Auth Tag extension enables receivers to verify that object content originated from the publisher, regardless of how many relay hops it traversed.

Auth on repair objects is particularly important: a compromised relay could inject malicious repair symbols that cause receivers to reconstruct corrupted source data after FEC recovery. When auth is enabled, receivers SHOULD verify repair object auth tags before feeding symbols to the FEC decoder.

section.name: Single-Group Blocks (Interleave Depth = 1)
section.t: When Interleave Depth is 1, each FEC block corresponds to exactly one MoQ Group:
section.artwork: Group 0: [Obj 0] [Obj 1] ... [Obj K-1] -> FEC Block 0 Group 1: [Obj 0] [Obj 1] ... [Obj K-1] -> FEC Block 1 ...
section.t: The Source Block Number (SBN) in repair objects equals the Group ID.

section.name: Multi-Group Blocks (Interleave Depth > 1)
section.t: When Interleave Depth is D > 1, each FEC block spans D consecutive Groups:
section.artwork: Groups 0-3 (D=4): -> FEC Block 0, SBN = 0 Groups 4-7 (D=4): -> FEC Block 1, SBN = 1 ...
section.t: The SBN is computed as: SBN = floor(Group_ID / Interleave_Depth)
The first Group ID covered by a block is: First_Group = SBN * Interleave_Depth
Repair objects for Block N are published after the last source object of Group (N+1) * D - 1.

section.name: Interleaving
section.t: Interleaving spreads source symbols across time to protect against burst loss. With interleave depth D, symbols from D consecutive media units are grouped into one source block:
section.artwork: Media Units: M0 M1 M2 M3 M4 M5 M6 M7 | | | | | | | | Interleave=4: [--- Block 0 ---] [--- Block 1 ---] S0 S1 S2 S3 S4 S5 S6 S7 | | Block 0 Repair: Block 1 Repair: R0, R1 R2, R3
section.t: A burst loss affecting M1-M2 loses symbols S1,S2 from Block 0. With P >= 2 repair symbols, Block 0 can still be recovered.
Publishers SHOULD choose interleave depth based on:

ul.li: Expected burst loss duration
Acceptable recovery latency
Available bandwidth for repair overhead

section.t: Typical values:

section.name: Priority and Congestion
section.t: Repair tracks SHOULD use lower priority than source tracks so that repair data is dropped first under congestion.
Recommended priority assignment:
Publishers set priority via the QUIC stream priority mechanism or MoQ-specific priority signaling.
Under congestion, this priority separation ensures:

ol.li: Source media is preserved as long as possible
Repair overhead is gracefully shed
Receivers degrade to QUIC retransmission when FEC is unavailable

section.name: Hybrid Unicast and Multicast Delivery
section.t: This FEC mechanism is designed to support hybrid delivery architectures where the same media stream is delivered via multiple transport paths simultaneously. Multicast delivery paths (Section 3), TreeDN integration (Section 5), and transport hierarchy (Section 6) are defined in ; this section covers FEC-specific considerations for hybrid delivery.

section.name: Multicast and AMT Delivery
section.t: IP multicast (including Source-Specific Multicast per ) and Automatic Multicast Tunneling (AMT per ) provide efficient one-to-many delivery but are inherently unreliable. FEC enables receivers to recover from packet loss without retransmission:

ul.li: Native SSM: Receivers join multicast group directly
AMT Tunneling: Receivers without native multicast connect via AMT relays discovered through DRIAD ()

TreeDN Distribution: Hierarchical multicast trees per with FEC protection at each hop. ISP edge routers with inline AMT relay capability can serve as TreeDN nodes, replacing x86 CDN servers with in-router replication while preserving FEC repair across the delivery path

section.t: The same MoQ objects (source and repair) can be transmitted over both unicast (QUIC/WebTransport) and multicast (UDP/SSM/AMT) paths. Receivers on reliable unicast paths MAY skip FEC decoding, while receivers on lossy multicast paths use FEC for recovery.
section.artwork:
Publisher | v +----------------+ | MoQ Relay | | (Origin/Root) | +-------+--------+ | +----+----+--------------------+ | | | v v v Unicast SSM Multicast AMT Tunnel (QUIC) (Native) (Encapsulated) | | | v v v [Web] [Native SSM [Mobile/ Client Clients] Remote]
FEC: FEC: FEC: Optional Required Required

section.name: Relay-Generated Repair
section.t: Relays MAY generate additional repair symbols for local network conditions. This is enabled by the fountain code property of RaptorQ, which allows generation of arbitrary repair symbols from decoded source data.
Use cases for relay-generated repair include:

ul.li: Edge relays serving lossy last-mile networks (WiFi, cellular) can increase repair overhead beyond what the publisher provides
Regional relays can adapt FEC parameters to local loss patterns
Relays can regenerate repair if upstream repair symbols were lost
IWA home gateways receiving multicast over wired Ethernet can apply local FEC repair before redistributing via WebTransport to wireless clients, reducing retransmission round trips to the origin

section.t: When a relay generates its own repair symbols:

ol.li: The relay MUST successfully decode the source block first
Generated repair symbols SHOULD use ESIs that do not conflict with publisher-generated repair (e.g., ESI >= K + P_publisher)
The relay MAY advertise relay-specific repair via a separate track namespace or track path suffix

section.t: Security considerations for relay-generated repair:

A compromised relay could inject malicious repair symbols that cause receivers to reconstruct corrupted source data. This risk is elevated on multicast paths where QUIC's integrity guarantees are absent. To mitigate:

ol.li: Block-level integrity: Publishers SHOULD use the Auth Tag extension () on source objects so receivers can verify that decoded source data matches the publisher's original after FEC recovery.

Trust model: Receivers MUST distinguish between publisher-generated repair (trusted, from the origin) and relay-generated repair (semi-trusted). If decoded data fails integrity verification using relay-generated repair, receivers SHOULD discard it and request retransmission via unicast.
Separate tracks: Relay-generated repair SHOULD be published on a distinct track (e.g., "video/repair/relay") so receivers can apply different trust policies.

section.artwork: Publisher (K=32, P=8, 25% overhead) | | Source ESI: 0-31, Repair ESI: 32-39 v +------------------+ | Backbone Relay | (passes through, low loss) +--------+---------+ | +----+----+ | | v v +--------+ +--------+ | Edge A | | Edge B | | P=16 | | P=4 | | (50%) | | (12%) | +--------+ +--------+ | | v v [Lossy [Clean WiFi] Fiber]

section.name: Multi-Path FEC Delivery
section.t: When source and repair data are delivered via multiple transport paths simultaneously (e.g., MoQ/QUIC unicast and SSM multicast, or MoQ/QUIC and ATSC 3.0 RF broadcast), receivers combine FEC symbols from all available paths:
section.artwork: +------ MoQ/QUIC (reliable) ------+ | | Publisher --> MoQ Relay Receiver | | +------ SSM Multicast (lossy) ----+ or ATSC 3.0 RF broadcast
section.t: Key multi-path considerations:

ol.li: Format consistency: All paths delivering repair symbols for the same source track SHOULD use the same repair container format. This ensures the receiver applies one byte-offset extraction recipe per track, branching once on container type at subscription time.

Deduplication: Receivers receiving the same source symbol via multiple paths MUST deduplicate before FEC decoding. Source Block Number (SBN) and Encoding Symbol ID (ESI) uniquely identify each symbol across all paths.

Per-packet OTI: When using container formats that carry OTI per-packet (e.g., MMTP, ), receivers that join mid-stream obtain OTI from the first repair packet received on any path, without waiting for FEC_CONFIG delivery.

Reliable + unreliable combination: Source symbols on the reliable MoQ/QUIC path and repair symbols on the lossy multicast path is a valid configuration. Receivers on multi-path MAY skip FEC decoding when the reliable path delivers all source symbols, using repair only when the reliable path experiences elevated loss or congestion.

section.name: ATSC 3.0 Compatibility
section.t: This specification is designed for interoperability with ATSC A/331 Application-Layer FEC.

section.name: Mapping to A/331 AL-FEC

tr.td: Derived from Interleave Depth x frame duration

section.t: Publishers ingesting ATSC 3.0 broadcasts SHOULD preserve the original FEC parameters and pass them through in FEC_CONFIG.
For ATSC 3.0 MMT mode ingest, the repair track uses "mmtp" packaging -- repair packets are passed through as-is. See for the MMTP repair format and multi-path deployment scenarios.

section.name: S-TSID to FEC_CONFIG Conversion
section.t: When converting ATSC S-TSID FEC parameters to MoQ FEC_CONFIG:
section.artwork:
S-TSID (per ATSC A/331): <FECParameters maximumDelay="1000" overhead="25" fecOTI="F=32;T=1312;Z=4;N=1;Al=8"> <ProtectedObject tsi="1"> <SourceTOI x="0" y="255"/> </ProtectedObject> </FECParameters>
Note: The fecOTI attribute uses ATSC A/331 field names, which differ from RFC 6330 terminology. F denotes the number of source symbols per block (equivalent to K in this document), T is symbol size, Z is the number of source blocks, N is sub-blocks, and Al is symbol alignment. RFC 6330 uses F for Transfer Length (40 bits), which is a different concept; implementors should use the A/331 schema as the reference for S-TSID parsing.
Converts to FEC_CONFIG: FEC Algorithm: 0x02 (RaptorQ) Source Symbols Per Block: 32 (from F parameter) Repair Symbols Per Block: 8 (25% of 32, from overhead) Symbol Size: 1312 (from T parameter) Interleave Depth: 4 (from Z parameter or derived from maximumDelay) OTI: [12-byte concatenation of Common FEC OTI + Scheme-Specific FEC OTI per RFC 6330]

section.name: Interaction with CMAF Packaging
section.t: This specification is designed to work alongside CMAF packaging for MoQ . The relationship is:
FEC encoding is applied to CMAF chunk payloads, treating each chunk (or portion thereof) as source symbols. The FEC layer is agnostic to the media container format.

section.name: CARP Integration
section.t: The CMAF-Aware Real-time Protocol (CARP) uses the same FEC catalog fields () and multicast endpoint format ( Section 7) as any other MoQ packaging format. No CARP-specific FEC signaling is needed -- CARP catalogs include the "fec" and "multicast" objects defined in this document and respectively.
CMAF source objects with FEC enabled MUST carry the FEC Payload ID and Object Length extension headers (, ). CARP receivers use these extensions identically to non-CARP CMAF receivers.

section.name: FEC Does Not Provide Confidentiality
section.t: FEC repair symbols are derived from source data. If source data is encrypted, repair symbols will also be encrypted by virtue of operating on ciphertext. However, FEC itself provides no confidentiality guarantees.

section.name: Repair Symbol Manipulation
section.t: An attacker who can modify repair symbols could cause receivers to reconstruct incorrect source data. Integrity protection of repair tracks is RECOMMENDED, using the same mechanisms as source tracks (e.g., QUIC packet protection).

section.name: Amplification
section.t: FEC repair symbols represent additional bandwidth. Publishers MUST NOT generate excessive repair symbols that could be used for bandwidth amplification attacks. The repair overhead (P/K) SHOULD be limited to reasonable values (e.g., <= 50%).

section.name: Multicast FEC Security
section.t: When FEC is used over multicast (SSM/AMT), additional considerations apply:

ol.li: No QUIC Protection: Multicast UDP lacks QUIC's integrity guarantees. Publishers SHOULD use DTLS-SRTP or equivalent for payload encryption when confidentiality is required.

Repair Injection: Attackers could inject malicious repair symbols on multicast networks. Receivers SHOULD verify FEC block consistency by checking that decoded data matches expected patterns (e.g., valid media container structure).
Source Authentication: For SSM, receivers verify source address matches (S,G) subscription. For AMT, trust is delegated to the AMT relay's authentication mechanisms.

Block Integrity: Receivers MAY implement block-level checksums to detect corrupted recovery. If decoded source data fails integrity checks, receivers SHOULD request retransmission via unicast fallback.

section.name: MoQ Message Type
section.t: This document requests registration of a new MoQ message type in the "MoQ Message Types" registry:

Note: The value 0x50 is tentative and subject to coordination with the MOQ WG chairs to avoid collision with other allocations. Implementations SHOULD use a value in the experimental range (0xF000-0xFFFF) until IANA allocation is confirmed. This registration is shared with , which references this document for the normative FEC_CONFIG definition.

section.name: MoQ Extension Header Type
section.t: This document requests registration of a new MoQ extension header type in a separate "MoQ Extension Header Types" registry (note: this is a different IANA registry than the message type registry in , so reuse of the value 0x50 does not create a collision):

section.name: FEC Algorithm Registry
section.t: This document requests creation of a "MoQ FEC Algorithms" registry with the following initial values:
New registrations require Specification Required policy.

section.name: MoQ Streaming Format Packaging
section.t: This document requests registration of a MoQ Streaming Format packaging value in the registry established by :

The "condensed" packaging is used for CMAF repair tracks where the source packaging (cmaf) does not have native FEC framing. Each MoQ object contains one raw repair symbol with SBN/ESI derived from MoQ transport framing ().
Note: The "mmtp" packaging value is registered by . The "loc" packaging value is registered by . Repair tracks using those packagings follow the respective format's native FEC framing.

section.name: MoQ Object Extension Header Types
section.t: This document requests registration of the following MoQ object extension header types in the "MoQ Extension Header Types" registry (note: these are object-level extensions per , distinct from the control message extension in ):

Note: The values 0xNN are placeholders. Actual values are to be assigned in coordination with the MOQ WG. Implementations SHOULD use values in the experimental range until IANA allocation is confirmed.

front.title: Key words for use in RFCs to Indicate Requirement Levels
Reed-Solomon Forward Error Correction (FEC) Schemes
RaptorQ Forward Error Correction Scheme for Object Delivery
Ambiguity of Uppercase vs Lowercase in RFC 2119 Key Words
QUIC: A UDP-Based Multiplexed and Secure Transport
Media over QUIC Transport
Low Overhead Media Container
Common Catalog Format for MoQ
CMSF: A CMAF Compliant Implementation of MOQT Streaming Format
MPEG Media Transport (MMT) Packaging for Media over QUIC
Multicast Delivery and Endpoint Discovery for Media over QUIC
CMAF-Aware Real-time Protocol
Authentication for Loss-Tolerant Anycast
Source-Specific Multicast for IP
Automatic Multicast Tunneling
DNS Reverse IP Automatic Multicast Tunneling (AMT) Discovery
TreeDN: Tree-Based Content Delivery Network (CDN) for Live Streaming to Mass Audiences
Signaling, Delivery, Synchronization, and Error Protection
MMT-Based Media Transport Scheme in Digital Broadcasting Systems
Information technology - High efficiency coding and media delivery in heterogeneous environments - Part 1: MPEG media transport (MMT)
TreeDN - The Fix for Catastrophically Successful Live Streaming Events

section.name: Recovery Example
section.t: Subscriber receives source objects 0,1,3,4,5,6,7,8,9 (object 2 lost) and repair objects 0,1,2.
With K=10, P=3, subscriber has:

ul.li: 9 source symbols (ESI 0,1,3,4,5,6,7,8,9)
3 repair symbols (ESI 10,11,12)
Total: 12 symbols >= K=10

section.t: RaptorQ decoder can recover the missing source symbol (ESI 2).

section.name: Per-Object Overhead Comparison
section.t: The following table compares per-object FEC signaling overhead across container formats. "MoQ Extensions (CMAF+FEC)" uses the extension headers defined in .

tr.td: Auth Tag extension only (N from catalog)
Raw symbol payload + Auth Tag extension
FEC Payload ID (8) + Object Length (1-4 varint)
FEC PID + Object Length + Auth Tag
MMTP Header (12) + FEC Payload ID (9) + OTI (12)

section.t: For LOC source objects, FEC-specific overhead is zero -- SBN and ESI are derived from group_id and object_id (). However, if content authentication is enabled, the Auth Tag extension () adds N bytes per object as a MoQ extension header.

The MoQ Extensions format adds 9-12 bytes per CMAF source object: 8 bytes for the FEC Payload ID extension (SBN+ESI) and 1-4 bytes for the Object Length extension (QUIC varint encoding -- 1 byte for objects up to 63 bytes, 2 bytes up to 16383, 4 bytes up to ~1GB). At typical CMAF video rates (30fps, interleave depth 4, ~7.5 objects/second), this is approximately 68-90 bytes/second.
The MMTP format carries full per-packet OTI for self-describing operation and ATSC 3.0 compatibility.

section.name: Catalog Example
section.t: Complete catalog with FEC and multicast configuration, showing all three packaging types with their corresponding repair tracks:
The "multicast" field uses the simple format defined in Section 7.1. For multi-endpoint deployments, the extended format (Section 7.2) with "endpoints" array is also available.

section.name: MMTP Repair Container Mapping (Informational)
section.t: NOTE: This appendix is informational. It creates no normative dependency on . Implementations that do not interwork with MMTP sources need not implement this packaging type.

section.name: Motivation
section.t: MMTP repair packaging enables FEC interoperability between MoQ CDN delivery and MMTP-based broadcast systems including SSM/ASM multicast and ATSC 3.0 MMT mode . The repair object payload is an unmodified MMTP repair packet per , enabling receivers to apply identical FEC recovery regardless of delivery path.

In hybrid architectures, the same content may arrive via multiple paths simultaneously. The receiver does not need to distinguish which path delivered a given repair packet -- all paths produce byte-identical MMTP payloads:
The MMTP rows share one repair format across broadcast and CDN paths. The condensed rows use the lightweight format () for pure-MoQ deployments. LOC repair uses LOC packaging ().

section.name: Wire Format
section.t: When the repair track's packaging is "mmtp", each MoQ repair object payload contains one complete MMTP repair packet:

section.name: Receiver Processing
section.t: The receiver extracts FEC parameters by reading at fixed byte offsets. This requires one branch on the repair container type (determined at subscription time from FEC_CONFIG or catalog), then fixed-offset integer reads and a memcpy of symbol data:
section.artwork: offset 0: MMTP Header (12 bytes) -- skip offset 12: SS_Start (3 bytes, big-endian) -> Source Block Number offset 15: RSB_length (3 bytes) -- skip offset 18: RS_ID (3 bytes) -> Encoding Symbol ID derivation offset 21: OTI (12 bytes) -> RFC 6330 Common + Scheme-Specific OTI offset 21: Transfer Length F (5 bytes, 40-bit big-endian) offset 26: Reserved (1 byte) offset 27: Symbol Size T (2 bytes, big-endian) offset 29: Num Source Blocks Z (1 byte) offset 30: Num Sub-Blocks N (2 bytes) offset 32: Alignment Al (1 byte) offset 33: Repair Symbol Data (T bytes) -> memcpy to decoder
section.t: K (source symbols per block) is derived: K = ceil(F / T).

section.name: Per-Packet OTI
section.t: Unlike the condensed format () where repair objects carry only the raw symbol data and OTI is signaled once via FEC_CONFIG, the MMTP container carries OTI in every repair packet (12 bytes at offset 21). This provides:

ul.li: Immediate OTI availability when joining mid-stream via any path
Robustness to FEC_CONFIG loss or late delivery
Support for mixed-K streams (e.g., video K=32, audio K=4) on a shared repair track, where per-packet OTI disambiguates

section.t: The overhead is 12 bytes per repair symbol. At typical rates (8 repair symbols/block x ~7.5 blocks/second for 30fps video with depth=4), this is ~720 bytes/second -- negligible relative to video bitrate.

section.name: Relay Behavior
section.t: MoQ relays ingesting MMTP sources (multicast or direct) SHOULD pass repair packets through without modification:

ol.li: Receive MMTP repair packet (FEC Type=2)
Publish as MoQ object on the repair track
Set Repair Container to 0x01 in FEC_CONFIG

section.t: No header stripping, no re-framing, no re-encoding.

section.name: Relationship to FEC_CONFIG
section.t: When Repair Container is 0x01:

ul.li: FEC_CONFIG is OPTIONAL (OTI is per-packet)
If sent, FEC_CONFIG fields (K, P, T, depth) are informational and SHOULD match the per-packet OTI values
FEC_CONFIG is still useful for repair track discovery and to signal FEC availability before the first repair packet arrives
